heading: War crimes
label: 2441 War crimes

Implementation of Treaty Obligations, , , provided that:Pub. L. 109–366, § 6(a)120 Stat. 2632Oct. 17, 2006“(1).—In generalThe acts enumerated in subsection (d) of , United States Code, as added by subsection (b) of this section, and in subsection (c) of this section [enacting , The Public Health and Welfare], constitute violations of common Article 3 of the Geneva Conventions prohibited by United States law.section 2441 of title 18section 2000dd–0 of Title 42“(2).—Prohibition on grave breachesThe provisions of , United States Code, as amended by this section, fully satisfy the obligation under Article 129 of the Third Geneva Convention for the United States to provide effective penal sanctions for grave breaches which are encompassed in common Article 3 in the context of an armed conflict not of an international character. No foreign or international source of law shall supply a basis for a rule of decision in the courts of the United States in interpreting the prohibitions enumerated in subsection (d) of such section 2441.section 2441 of title 18“(3)Interpretation by the president.—“(A)As provided by the Constitution and by this section, the President has the authority for the United States to interpret the meaning and application of the Geneva Conventions and to promulgate higher standards and administrative regulations for violations of treaty obligations which are not grave breaches of the Geneva Conventions.“(B)The President shall issue interpretations described by subparagraph (A) by Executive Order published in the Federal Register.“(C)Any Executive Order published under this paragraph shall be authoritative (except as to grave breaches of common Article 3) as a matter of United States law, in the same manner as other administrative regulations.“(D)Nothing in this section shall be construed to affect the constitutional functions and responsibilities of Congress and the judicial branch of the United States.“(4).—DefinitionsIn this subsection:“(A).—Geneva conventionsThe term ‘Geneva Conventions’ means—“(i)the Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded and Sick in Armed Forces in the Field, done at Geneva (6 UST 3217);August 12, 1949“(ii)the Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded, Sick, and Shipwrecked Members of the Armed Forces at Sea, done at Geneva (6 UST 3217);August 12, 1949“(iii)the Convention Relative to the Treatment of Prisoners of War, done at Geneva (6 UST 3316); andAugust 12, 1949“(iv)the Convention Relative to the Protection of Civilian Persons in Time of War, done at Geneva (6 UST 3516).August 12, 1949“(B).—Third geneva conventionThe term ‘Third Geneva Convention’ means the international convention referred to in subparagraph (A)(iii).”

children.heading: .—Offense
children.label: a .—Offense
children.text: Whoever, whether inside or outside the United States, commits a war crime, in any of the circumstances described in subsection (b), shall be fined under this title or imprisoned for life or any term of years, or both, and if death results to the victim, shall also be subject to the penalty of death.
the offense occurs in whole or in part within the United States; or

children.label: i
children.text: a national of the United States or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence; or

children.label: ii
children.text: a member of the Armed Forces of the United States, regardless of nationality; or

children.label: B
children.text: the offender is present in the United States, regardless of the nationality of the victim or offender.
defined as a grave breach in any of the international conventions signed at Geneva 12 August 1949, or any protocol to such convention to which the United States is a party;
prohibited by Article 23, 25, 27, or 28 of the Annex to the Hague Convention IV, Respecting the Laws and Customs of War on Land, signed 18 October 1907;
which constitutes a grave breach of common Article 3 (as defined in subsection (d)) when committed in the context of and in association with an armed conflict not of an international character; or

of a person who, in relation to an armed conflict and contrary to the provisions of the Protocol on Prohibitions or Restrictions on the Use of Mines, Booby-Traps and Other Devices as amended at Geneva on 3 May 1996 (Protocol II as amended on 3 May 1996), when the United States is a party to such Protocol, willfully kills or causes serious injury to civilians.

children.heading: .—Torture
children.label: A .—Torture
children.text: The act of a person who commits, or conspires or attempts to commit, an act specifically intended to inflict severe physical or mental pain or suffering (other than pain or suffering incidental to lawful sanctions) upon another person within his custody or physical control for the purpose of obtaining information or a confession, punishment, intimidation, coercion, or any reason based on discrimination of any kind.

children.heading: .—Cruel or inhuman treatment
children.label: B .—Cruel or inhuman treatment
children.text: The act of a person who commits, or conspires or attempts to commit, an act intended to inflict severe or serious physical or mental pain or suffering (other than pain or suffering incidental to lawful sanctions), including serious physical abuse, upon another within his custody or control.

children.heading: .—Performing biological experiments
children.label: C .—Performing biological experiments
children.text: The act of a person who subjects, or conspires or attempts to subject, one or more persons within his custody or physical control to biological experiments without a legitimate medical or dental purpose and in so doing endangers the body or health of such person or persons.

children.heading: .—Murder
children.label: D .—Murder
children.text: The act of a person who intentionally kills, or conspires or attempts to kill, or kills whether intentionally or unintentionally in the course of committing any other offense under this subsection, one or more persons taking no active part in the hostilities, including those placed out of combat by sickness, wounds, detention, or any other cause.

children.heading: .—Mutilation or maiming
children.label: E .—Mutilation or maiming
children.text: The act of a person who intentionally injures, or conspires or attempts to injure, or injures whether intentionally or unintentionally in the course of committing any other offense under this subsection, one or more persons taking no active part in the hostilities, including those placed out of combat by sickness, wounds, detention, or any other cause, by disfiguring the person or persons by any mutilation thereof or by permanently disabling any member, limb, or organ of his body, without any legitimate medical or dental purpose.

children.heading: .—Intentionally causing serious bodily injury
children.label: F .—Intentionally causing serious bodily injury
children.text: The act of a person who intentionally causes, or conspires or attempts to cause, serious bodily injury to one or more persons, including lawful combatants, in violation of the law of war.

children.heading: .—Rape
children.label: G .—Rape
children.text: The act of a person who forcibly or with coercion or threat of force wrongfully invades, or conspires or attempts to invade, the body of a person by penetrating, however slightly, the anal or genital opening of the victim with any part of the body of the accused, or with any foreign object.

children.heading: .—Sexual assault or abuse
children.label: H .—Sexual assault or abuse
children.text: The act of a person who forcibly or with coercion or threat of force engages, or conspires or attempts to engage, in sexual contact with one or more persons, or causes, or conspires or attempts to cause, one or more persons to engage in sexual contact.

children.heading: .—Taking hostages
children.label: I .—Taking hostages
children.text: The act of a person who, having knowingly seized or detained one or more persons, threatens to kill, injure, or continue to detain such person or persons with the intent of compelling any nation, person other than the hostage, or group of persons to act or refrain from acting as an explicit or implicit condition for the safety or release of such person or persons.

children.label: A
children.text: the term “severe mental pain or suffering” shall be applied for purposes of paragraphs (1)(A) and (1)(B) in accordance with the meaning given that term in ;section 2340(2) of this title

children.label: B
children.text: the term “serious bodily injury” shall be applied for purposes of paragraph (1)(F) in accordance with the meaning given that term in ;section 113(b)(2) of this title

children.label: C
children.text: the term “sexual contact” shall be applied for purposes of paragraph (1)(G) in accordance with the meaning given that term in ;section 2246(3) of this title

children.label: i
children.text: a substantial risk of death;

children.label: ii
children.text: extreme physical pain;

children.label: iii
children.text: a burn or physical disfigurement of a serious nature (other than cuts, abrasions, or bruises); or

children.label: iv
children.text: significant loss or impairment of the function of a bodily member, organ, or mental faculty; and

children.label: i
children.text: the term “serious” shall replace the term “severe” where it appears; and

children.label: ii
children.text: as to conduct occurring after the date of the enactment of the Military Commissions Act of 2006, the term “serious and non-transitory mental harm (which need not be prolonged)” shall replace the term “prolonged mental harm” where it appears.

children.heading: .—Inapplicability of certain provisions with respect to collateral damage or incident of lawful attack
children.label: 3 .—Inapplicability of certain provisions with respect to collateral damage or incident of lawful attack
A
children.text: collateral damage; or

children.label: B
children.text: death, damage, or injury incident to a lawful attack.

children.heading: .—Inapplicability of taking hostages to prisoner exchange
children.label: 4 .—Inapplicability of taking hostages to prisoner exchange
children.text: Paragraph (1)(I) does not apply to an offense under subsection (a) by reason of subsection (c)(3) in the case of a prisoner exchange during wartime.

children.heading: .—Definition of grave breaches
children.label: 5 .—Definition of grave breaches
children.text: The definitions in this subsection are intended only to define the grave breaches of common Article 3 and not the full scope of United States obligations under that Article.

children.heading: .—Nonapplicability of Certain Limitations
children.label: e .—Nonapplicability of Certain Limitations
children.text: In the case of an offense described in subsection (a) and further described in subsections (c)(1) and (c)(3), an indictment may be found or an information may be instituted at any time without limitation.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: No prosecution for an offense described in subsection (a) shall be undertaken by the United States except on written certification of the Attorney General, the Deputy Attorney General, or an Assistant Attorney General, which function of approving prosecutions may not be delegated, that a prosecution by the United States is in the public interest and necessary to secure substantial justice.

children.label: A
children.text: whether the alleged offender can be removed from the United States for purposes of prosecution in another jurisdiction; and

children.label: B
children.text: potential adverse consequences for nationals, servicemembers, or employees of the United States.

children.heading: .—Input From Other Agency Heads
children.label: g .—Input From Other Agency Heads
children.text: The Secretary of Defense and Secretary of State may submit to the Attorney General for consideration their views generally regarding potential benefits, or potential adverse consequences for nationals, servicemembers, or employees of the United States, of prosecutions of offenses for which jurisdiction exists under subsection (b)(2)(B).

children.heading: .—No Judicial Review
children.label: h .—No Judicial Review
children.text: Certifications under subsection (f) and input from other agency heads under subsection (g) are not subject to judicial review.
support for ratification of or accession to the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court, which entered into force on ; orJuly 1, 2002
consent by the United States to any assertion or exercise of jurisdiction by any international, hybrid, or foreign court.
